# Pest and Disease Control

## Common Pests in Portland Tomato Gardens

### Aphids

- **Identification**: Small soft-bodied insects (green, black, or white) clustered on new growth and undersides of leaves. Produce sticky honeydew that attracts ants.
- **Organic control**: Strong water spray to knock off colonies. Release ladybugs (1,500 count from Arbico Organics, $14) in early evening. Spray neem oil (Bonide brand, diluted per label: 2 tbsp per gallon) weekly as preventive starting in May.
- **Companion planting**: Interplant with nasturtiums as trap crop. Aphids prefer nasturtiums and will congregate there instead of tomatoes.

### Tomato Hornworm (Manduca quinquemaculata)

- **Identification**: Large green caterpillar (up to 4 inches), with white diagonal stripes and a horn on the rear. Devastates foliage rapidly.
- **Organic control**: Hand-pick daily during July and August (check undersides of leaves in early morning). Apply Bacillus thuringiensis (BT) spray (Monterey BT brand, kurstaki strain) every 7-10 days starting when first caterpillar is spotted. Do NOT remove hornworms that have white cocoons (braconid wasp parasites) — these beneficial wasps are controlling the population.
- **Notes**: Kevin found 7 hornworms on Cherokee Purples in August 2025. Damage was significant before he noticed them. Plan to inspect plants every other day starting July 1.

### Slugs

- **Identification**: Slimy trails on leaves, irregular holes in low-hanging fruit
- **Organic control**: Sluggo (iron phosphate pellets) scattered around bed perimeters every 2 weeks during wet periods. Beer traps (shallow containers buried at soil level). Copper tape around raised bed edges.

## Common Diseases

### Late Blight (Phytophthora infestans)

- **Risk**: Moderate to high in Portland's wet climate, especially in September/October
- **Prevention**: Provide good air circulation (prune lower leaves, 24-inch spacing for indeterminates). Avoid overhead watering (drip irrigation only). Apply copper fungicide (Bonide Liquid Copper) preventively starting mid-August if wet weather is forecast.
- **If detected**: Remove and bag affected foliage immediately. Do NOT compost — dispose in trash.

### Blossom End Rot

- **Cause**: Calcium deficiency in developing fruit, often triggered by inconsistent watering rather than actual low soil calcium
- **Prevention**: Maintain consistent soil moisture through drip irrigation and mulching. Apply gypsum at planting time (see soil-prep.md). Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which promotes leaf growth over fruit development.
- **Especially watch**: Cherokee Purple is highly susceptible. In 2025, the Lius lost approximately 30% of Cherokee Purple fruit to BER.

## Companion Planting Strategy

- **Basil** (Genovese): Planted between tomato plants in Bed 3. Reputed to repel aphids and whiteflies. Also useful in kitchen.
- **Marigolds** (French dwarf, Tagetes patula): Planted at ends of every bed. Roots exude alpha-terthienyl which suppresses root-knot nematodes. Choose single-flower types that attract hoverflies (aphid predators).
- **Borage**: One plant per bed. Attracts pollinators, deters hornworms, and self-seeds reliably.
